summary: Companies at scale should invest in specialized in-house expertise even in areas not traditionally considered 'core' to their business, because these investments pay for themselves many times over through incident mitigation, performance optimizations, and long-term cost savings. Twitter's kernel team and JVM experts are cited as examples where single optimizations (like a 5-15% performance improvement from virtualizing compare-and-swap operations) generated returns far exceeding team costs in perpetuity. The common advice to 'buy vs build' and focus only on 'core competencies' is oversimplified—companies can strategically choose their comparative advantages and develop expertise that becomes core, as Apple did with CPU design.
key_points: Twitter's kernel team helped quickly debug a critical firewall/kernel bug that caused the company's worst outage since 2013
A JVM optimization by Twitter's Scala team reduced service costs by 5-15%, paying for the entire team in perpetuity
At sufficient scale, tiny percentage improvements (0.5% TCO reduction) justify specialized teams indefinitely
'Buy vs build' advice often fails because buying doesn't eliminate the need for in-house expertise to extract value
Companies can arbitrarily choose comparative advantages—Apple's CPU design wasn't inevitable before acquiring PA Semi for $278M
money_quotes: If, in the lifetime of the specialist team like the kernel team, a single person found something that persistently reduced TCO by 0.5%, that would pay for the team in perpetuity, and Twitter's kernel team has found many such changes.

The two patches combined reduced the cost of typical major Twitter services using idiomatic Scala by 5% to 15%, paying for the JVM team in perpetuity many times over and that wasn't even the biggest win Flavio found that year.
While it's technically possible to hire yet another firm to be the expert, that's more expensive than developing or hiring in-house expertise and, in the long run, also more risky.

There was nothing about Apple that made the purchase inevitable, that made CPU design an inherent comparative advantage of Apple. But if a firm can pick an area and make it an area of comparative advantage, saying that the firm should choose to concentrate on its comparative advantage(s) isn't very helpful advice.

Companies don't have to get all that large before it starts making sense to have in-house expertise in their operating system, language runtime, and other components that people often think of as being fairly specialized.
key_insight: The conventional wisdom to outsource non-core functions and focus on comparative advantage ignores that at scale, companies can profitably develop deep expertise in 'specialized' areas like kernels and JVMs, with single optimizations often paying for entire teams in perpetuity while also choosing what becomes their comparative advantage.
